chunks.title: COMMUNICATION FROM THE COMMISSION
chunks.subtitle: A Union of Equality: Gender Equality Strategy 2020-2025
chunks.content: **A Union of Equality: Gender Equality Strategy 2020-2025**

chunks.title: TOWARDS A GENDER-EQUAL EUROPE
chunks.content:
"In all its activities, the Union shall aim to eliminate inequalities, and to promote equality, between men and women."
Article 8 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union
"We should not be shy about being proud of where we are or ambitious about where we want to go."
- President Ursula von der Leyen Political Guidelines
The promotion of equality between women and men is a task for the Union, in all its activities, required by the Treaties. Gender equality is a core value of the EU, a fundamental right and key principle of the European Pillar of Social Rights. It is a reflection of who we are. It is also an essential condition for an innovative, competitive and thriving European economy. In business, politics and society as a whole, we can only reach our full potential if we use all of our talent and diversity. Gender equality brings more jobs and higher productivity – a potential which needs to be realised as we embrace the green and digital transitions and face up to our demographic challenges.
The European Union is a global leader in gender equality: 14 of the top 20 countries worldwide on gender equality are EU Member States. Thanks to robust equal treatment legislation and jurisprudence, efforts to mainstream the gender perspective into different policy areas, and laws to address particular inequalities, the EU has made significant progress in gender equality in the last decades.
**However, no Member State has achieved full gender equality and progress is slow.** Member States on average scored 67.4 out of 100 in the EU Gender Equality Index 2019 , a score which has improved by just 5.4 points since 2005.

chunks.title: TOWARDS A GENDER-EQUAL EUROPE
chunks.content:
**Unfortunately progress with regard to gender equality is neither inevitable nor irreversible. We therefore need to give a new impetus to gender equality.** While the gender gap in education is being closed, gender gaps in employment, pay, care, power and pensions persist. Too many people still violate the principle of gender equality through sexist hate speech and by blocking action against gender-based violence and gender stereotypes. Gender-based violence and harassment continue at alarming levels. The #*MeToo* movement has demonstrated the extent of sexism and abuse that women and girls continue to face. At the same time, it has empowered women across the globe to now come forward with their experiences and bring cases to court.
**This Gender Equality Strategy frames the European Commission's work on gender equality and sets out the policy objectives and key actions for the 2020-2025 period** . It aims at achieving a gender equal Europe where gender-based violence, sex discrimination and structural inequality between women and men are a thing of the past. A Europe where women and men, girls and boys, in all their diversity , are equal. Where they are *free* to pursue their chosen path in life, where they have equal opportunities to *thrive*, and where they can equally participate in and *lead* our European society.
The implementation of this strategy will be based on the **dual approach** of targeted measures to achieve gender equality, combined with strengthened gender mainstreaming. The Commission will enhance gender mainstreaming by **systematically including a gender perspective in all stages of policy design in all EU policy areas, internal and external**. The strategy will be implemented **using intersectionality** – the combination of gender with other personal characteristics or identities, and how these intersections contribute to unique experiences of discrimination – **as a cross-cutting principle**.

chunks.title: TOWARDS A GENDER-EQUAL EUROPE
chunks.content:
In this year of 2020, which marks the 25th anniversary of the adoption of the Beijing Declaration and Platform for Action – the first universal commitment and action plan to advance on equality between women and men – **this strategy is the EU's contribution to shaping a better world for women and men, girls and boys**. It delivers on the gender equality Sustainable Development Goal (SDG 5), gender equality as a cross-cutting priority
 7 See European Institute for Gender Equality (EIGE): [https://eige.europa.eu/gender-equality-index/2019.](https://eige.europa.eu/gender-equality-index/2019)
of all SDGs, and on the EU's commitment to the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities.

chunks.title: 1. Being free from violence and stereotypes

qa_pairs.question: In which four environments does the EU Gender Equality Strategy assert that everyone must be protected from gender-based harm?
qa_pairs.answer: safe in their homes, close relationships, public spaces, online

qa_pairs.question: What societal construct must be dismantled so that individuals can freely pursue their educational and professional ambitions?
qa_pairs.answer: stereotypical gender norms

qa_pairs.question: What personal freedom, beyond physical safety, does the Strategy seek to guarantee for every person?
qa_pairs.answer: chosen educational and professional paths

qa_pairs.question: What contexts does the passage cover when describing where everyone should be safe?
qa_pairs.answer: The passage extends the requirement of safety to homes, close relationships, workplaces, public spaces, and the online environment. It thereby treats safety as a single condition that must hold across both private and public domains, including the digital one.

qa_pairs.question: How does the passage describe the freedom that women and men, girls and boys should enjoy?
qa_pairs.answer: According to the passage, individuals in all their diversity should be free to express their ideas and emotions and to pursue their chosen educational and professional paths. Freedom is therefore framed both expressively and vocationally rather than narrowly.

qa_pairs.question: Why does the introduction emphasise 'all their diversity' when referring to women and men, girls and boys?
qa_pairs.answer: By using the phrase 'all their diversity', the passage signals that the freedoms it describes apply to everyone within those broad categories without exception. It thus stresses that the chapter's protections are not limited to a homogeneous group.

qa_pairs.question: What role do stereotypical gender norms play in the passage's vision?
qa_pairs.answer: Stereotypical gender norms are presented as constraints that limit individuals' ability to pursue their chosen educational and professional paths. Removing those constraints is therefore framed as a precondition for the freedom the chapter envisages.

qa_pairs.question: How does the passage relate online environments to other settings when articulating its safety vision?
qa_pairs.answer: The passage places online safety alongside homes, close relationships, workplaces, and public spaces, treating the digital environment as one further domain where safety must be guaranteed. It is therefore not isolated as a separate or secondary concern.

qa_pairs.question: How are safety and free expression connected in the introductory passage?
qa_pairs.answer: The passage first establishes universal safety across personal, public, and online contexts, and then frames freedom to express ideas, emotions, and life choices as something that proceeds from that safety. The two are presented as complementary preconditions for pursuing one's chosen path.

chunks.content: Everyone should be safe in their homes, in their close relationships, in their workplaces, in public spaces, and online. Women and men, girls and boys, in all their diversity, should be free to express their ideas and emotions, and pursue their chosen educational and professional paths without the constraints of stereotypical gender norms.

chunks.title: 1. Being free from violence and stereotypes
chunks.subtitle: Ending gender-based violence

groups.label: Legal Instruments & EU Accession
spans: EU's accession to the Istanbul Convention

qa_pairs.question: What share of women in the EU have experienced physical and/or sexual violence since the age of 15?
qa_pairs.answer: 33% of women

qa_pairs.question: What proportion of women have suffered violence by an intimate partner?
qa_pairs.answer: 22% of women

qa_pairs.question: What fraction of women have been sexually harassed in a work or work-related context?
qa_pairs.answer: 55% of women

qa_pairs.question: Which international convention does the EU Gender Equality Strategy designate as the benchmark standard for combating violence against women?
qa_pairs.answer: Istanbul Convention

qa_pairs.question: Which Treaty provision would allow gender-based violence to be classified among the most serious cross-border offences in the EU?
qa_pairs.answer: Article 83(1) TFEU

qa_pairs.question: What category of serious EU-wide criminal offences could gender-based violence be added to under the Commission's proposed approach?
qa_pairs.answer: Eurocrimes

qa_pairs.question: Which two forms of gender violence mentioned in the Strategy fall under both criminal law and public health frameworks?
qa_pairs.answer: sexual harassment, female genital mutilation (FGM)

qa_pairs.question: How does the passage define gender-based violence?
qa_pairs.answer: Gender-based violence is defined as violence directed against a woman because she is a woman, or violence that affects women disproportionately. The passage describes it as deeply rooted in gender inequality and as one of society's biggest challenges.

qa_pairs.question: Why does the passage characterise gender-based violence as under-reported and overlooked?
qa_pairs.answer: It states that gender-based violence in all its forms remains under-reported and overlooked both inside and outside the EU, which the text links to its deep roots in gender inequality. This framing implies that structural factors keep many incidents hidden from view.

qa_pairs.question: Describe the EU's overall approach to gender-based violence as set out in the passage.
qa_pairs.answer: The EU commits to do all it can to prevent and combat gender-based violence, support and protect victims of such crimes, and hold perpetrators accountable for their behaviour. The approach therefore combines prevention, victim protection, and accountability rather than addressing any single dimension.

qa_pairs.question: What role is attributed to the Istanbul Convention in the EU's strategy?
qa_pairs.answer: The Istanbul Convention is presented as the benchmark for international standards on preventing and combating violence against women and domestic violence. The EU signed it in 2017, and concluding the EU's accession is described as a key priority for the Commission.

qa_pairs.question: Explain the purpose of the European Parliament's 2019 request for an opinion from the European Court of Justice.
qa_pairs.answer: The Parliament requested the opinion in order to accelerate the conclusion of the EU's accession to the Istanbul Convention. It is presented as a procedural step intended to move the accession process forward when progress had stalled.

qa_pairs.question: What contingency does the Commission envisage if EU accession to the Istanbul Convention remains blocked?
qa_pairs.answer: The Commission intends to propose measures in 2021, within the limits of EU competence, designed to achieve the same objectives as the Istanbul Convention. This presents a fallback route to the same substantive aims via EU legislation.

qa_pairs.question: Explain how the concept of 'Eurocrimes' is connected to combating gender-based violence in the passage.
qa_pairs.answer: Under Article 83(1) TFEU, certain serious cross-border crimes — known as Eurocrimes — can be subject to harmonisation at EU level. The Commission intends to present an initiative extending this list to specific forms of gender-based violence, thereby enabling EU-wide criminal law alignment in this area.

qa_pairs.question: How does the Commission plan to act on forms of violence already covered by existing Eurocrimes?
qa_pairs.answer: For violence already apprehended by existing Eurocrimes under Article 83(1) TFEU, the Commission will propose additional measures to prevent and combat them. The text identifies sexual harassment, abuse of women, and female genital mutilation as falling within this category.

qa_pairs.question: What overall picture of the prevalence of violence against women in the EU emerges from the figures cited in the passage?
qa_pairs.answer: The figures cited — 33% of women having experienced physical and/or sexual violence, 22% violence by an intimate partner, and 55% sexual harassment — together portray gender-based violence as widespread rather than exceptional. The passage uses them to underscore the scale of the problem the strategy addresses.

qa_pairs.question: How do EU accession to the Istanbul Convention, the 2021 measures, and the Eurocrimes initiative relate to each other?
qa_pairs.answer: Accession is presented as the preferred route, while the planned 2021 measures and the Eurocrimes initiative serve as complementary or fallback instruments within EU competence. Together they form a layered strategy aimed at securing the Convention's objectives even if accession itself is delayed.

chunks.content:
Gender-based violence – or violence that is directed against a woman because she is a woman or that affects women disproportionately – remains one of our societies' biggest challenges and is deeply rooted in gender inequality. Gender-based violence, in all its forms, remains under-reported and overlooked, both inside and outside the EU. The EU will do all it can to prevent and combat gender-based violence, support and protect victims of such crimes, and hold perpetrators accountable for their abusive behaviour.
**33% of women** in the EU have experienced physical and/or sexual violence.
**22% of women** in the EU have experienced violence by an intimate partner.
**55% of women** in the EU have been sexually harassed.
The Council of Europe Convention on preventing and combating violence against women and domestic violence – **the 'Istanbul Convention'** – is the benchmark for international standards in this field. The EU signed the Convention in 2017, and concluding the EU's accession is a key priority for the Commission. To accelerate the conclusion of the EU's accession, the European Parliament requested in 2019 an opinion from the European Court of Justice on this issue.
Should the EU's accession to the Istanbul Convention remain blocked, the Commission intends to propose in 2021 measures, within the limits of EU competence, to achieve the same objectives as the Istanbul Convention.
The Commission intends in particular to present an initiative with a view to **extending the areas of crime** where harmonisation is possible to specific forms of gender-based violence in accordance with Article 83(1) TFEU, the so-called **Eurocrimes**.
To the extent that they are already apprehended by the existing Eurocrimes within the meaning of Article 83(1) TFEU, the Commission will propose **additional measures to prevent and combat specific forms of gender-based violence**, including sexual harassment, abuse of women and female genital mutilation (FGM).

chunks.title: 1. Being free from violence and stereotypes
chunks.subtitle: Ending gender-based violence

groups.label: Prevention & Network Mechanisms
spans: EU network on the prevention of gender-based violence and domestic violence

groups.label: Individual
spans: women who have a health problem or disability

qa_pairs.question: Which five harmful traditional practices against women and girls does the EU Gender Equality Strategy explicitly commit to eliminating?
qa_pairs.answer: female genital mutilation, forced abortion, forced sterilisation, early and forced marriage, honour-related violence

qa_pairs.question: Which two EU instruments are proposed to strengthen support and protection for victims of gender-based violence?
qa_pairs.answer: Victims' Rights Strategy, Victims' Rights Directive

qa_pairs.question: What EU coordination mechanism is specifically devoted to sharing good practices on preventing domestic violence?
qa_pairs.answer: EU network on the prevention of gender-based violence and domestic violence

qa_pairs.question: What international labour organisation convention is referenced in the context of addressing violence and harassment in the world of work?
qa_pairs.answer: ILO Convention

qa_pairs.question: Which group of women facing compounded vulnerability is explicitly identified as requiring targeted support under the Strategy's victim protection measures?
qa_pairs.answer: women who have a health problem or disability

qa_pairs.question: How does the passage characterise practices such as female genital mutilation, forced abortion, and forced marriage?
qa_pairs.answer: These practices are characterised as forms of gender-based violence and as serious violations of women's and children's rights, both within the EU and around the world. They are thereby placed within the same overarching category as other violent acts the strategy targets.

qa_pairs.question: Explain the purpose of the planned Recommendation on the prevention of harmful practices.
qa_pairs.answer: The Recommendation is intended to set out effective pre-emptive measures and to acknowledge the importance of education in preventing harmful practices. It will also address strengthening of public services, prevention and support measures, capacity-building of professionals, and victim-centred access to justice.

qa_pairs.question: What is the role of the planned Victims' Rights Strategy described in the passage?
qa_pairs.answer: The Victims' Rights Strategy, to be presented in 2020, will address the specific needs of victims of gender-based violence, including domestic violence. It will build on the existing Victims' Rights Directive rather than replace it.

qa_pairs.question: Why does the passage single out women with a health problem or disability for specific measures?
qa_pairs.answer: The passage notes that such women are more likely to experience various forms of violence, including abuse, forced sterilisation, and forced abortion. Targeted measures, including capacity-building of professionals and awareness-raising on rights and access to justice, are therefore planned to respond to that elevated risk.

qa_pairs.question: Describe the multi-disciplinary approach to violence prevention outlined in the passage.
qa_pairs.answer: Effective prevention is presented as requiring coordinated action across the criminal justice system, victim support services, perpetrator programmes, and social and health services. Education of boys and girls from an early age and the development of non-violent relationships are also presented as essential ingredients.

qa_pairs.question: How is the prevention of gender-based violence linked to the prevention of radicalisation in the passage?
qa_pairs.answer: The passage states that addressing violence against women and ideologies undermining women's rights could contribute to preventing radicalisation leading to violent extremism and terrorism. The two agendas are therefore framed as mutually reinforcing rather than separate.

qa_pairs.question: Explain the function of the planned EU network on the prevention of gender-based violence and domestic violence.
qa_pairs.answer: The network will bring together Member States and stakeholders to exchange good practice on preventing such violence. The Commission will also provide funding for training, capacity-building, and support services within this framework.

qa_pairs.question: Why does the passage place central importance on prevention focused on men, boys, and masculinities?
qa_pairs.answer: Because the passage frames violence against women as deeply rooted in gender inequality, it identifies men, boys, and ideas of masculinity as a key site for prevention work. Acting on these is therefore presented as essential rather than peripheral to ending violence.

qa_pairs.question: How does the passage approach violence and harassment in the workplace?
qa_pairs.answer: It states that the Commission will continue to encourage Member States to ratify the relevant ILO Convention on combating violence and harassment in the world of work. Workplace violence is thus addressed both through international labour standards and through the broader prevention agenda.

chunks.content:
Female genital mutilation, forced abortion and forced sterilisation, early and forced marriage, so-called 'honour-related violence' and other **harmful practices against women and girls are forms of gender-based violence and serious violations of women's and children's rights** within the EU and around the world. In addition to possible legislation, the EU will table a **Recommendation on the prevention of harmful practices**, including the need for effective pre-emptive measures and acknowledging the importance of education. The recommendation will also address the strengthening of public services, prevention and support measures, capacity-building of professionals and victim-centred access to justice.
The Commission will also present a **Victims**' **Rights Strategy** in 2020, which will address the specific needs of victims of gender-based violence, including domestic violence, building on the Victims' Rights Directive .
Women who have a **health problem or disability** are more likely to experience various forms of violence. The Commission will develop and finance measures to tackle abuse, violence as well as forced sterilisation and forced abortion, such as capacity-building of professionals and awareness-raising campaigns on rights and access to justice.
**Effective prevention of violence is key**. It involves educating boys and girls from an early age about gender equality and supporting the development of non-violent relationships. It also requires a multi-disciplinary approach among professionals and services including the criminal justice system, victim support services, perpetrator programmes and social and health services. Addressing violence against women and ideologies undermining women's rights could also contribute to the prevention of radicalisation leading to violent extremism and terrorism. The Commission will launch an **EU network on the prevention of genderbased violence and domestic violence**, bringing together Member States and stakeholders to exchange good practice, and will provide funding for training, capacity-building and support services. Violence prevention focusing on men, boys and masculinities will be of central importance.
To address violence and harassment in work contexts, the Commission will continue to encourage Member States to ratify the **International Labour Organisation (ILO)**

groups.label: Online Violence & Platform Policy
spans: new framework for cooperation between internet platforms

groups.label: Trafficking & Sexual Exploitation
spans: EU strategy on the eradication of trafficking in human beings

qa_pairs.question: What form of harm disproportionately affecting women in digital environments does the Strategy specifically commit to addressing?
qa_pairs.answer: Online violence targeting women

qa_pairs.question: Which legislative instrument does the Strategy propose to regulate platform accountability for combating online violence?
qa_pairs.answer: Digital Services Act

qa_pairs.question: What cross-border crime involving exploitation for commercial purposes does the Strategy commit to eradicating?
qa_pairs.answer: trafficking in human beings

qa_pairs.question: Which dedicated EU strategy will address the criminal phenomenon of trafficking for commercial exploitation?
qa_pairs.answer: EU strategy on the eradication of trafficking in human beings

qa_pairs.question: Explain the relationship between the ILO Convention and existing EU rules in the workplace context.
qa_pairs.answer: The Commission encourages ratification of the ILO Convention on violence and harassment in the world of work and, in parallel, calls for implementation of existing EU rules protecting workers from sexual harassment together with awareness-raising about them. The two instruments are presented as complementary tools tackling the same problem.

qa_pairs.question: Describe the legal framework the Commission, as an employer, intends to adopt against workplace harassment.
qa_pairs.answer: As an employer, the Commission will adopt a new comprehensive legal framework on harassment in the workplace, combining preventive and reactive measures. This is presented as the Commission leading by example within its own administration.

qa_pairs.question: How does the passage characterise the impact of online violence on women?
qa_pairs.answer: The passage describes online violence as pervasive and as having specific, vicious consequences, including being a barrier to women's participation in public life. Bullying, harassment, and abuse on social media are said to have far-reaching effects on women's and girls' daily lives.

qa_pairs.question: Explain the intended role of the Digital Services Act in protecting women online.
qa_pairs.answer: The Digital Services Act will clarify online platforms' responsibilities for user-disseminated content and what is expected of them in addressing illegal activities online while protecting fundamental rights. It is presented as the main legislative lever for tackling online violence against women.

qa_pairs.question: Why does the passage propose measures going beyond strictly illegal content?
qa_pairs.answer: It argues that users also need to be able to counter harmful and abusive content that is not always considered illegal but can still have devastating effects. The Commission therefore plans to facilitate the development of a new framework for cooperation between internet platforms in addition to its illegality-based measures.

qa_pairs.question: How does the passage describe the gender pattern of trafficking in human beings?
qa_pairs.answer: Women and girls are described as forming the vast majority of victims of trafficking in human beings, both in and outside the EU, and as being mostly trafficked for the purposes of sexual exploitation. The passage thus frames trafficking primarily as a gendered crime.

qa_pairs.question: Explain the strategic stance taken on impunity in trafficking cases.
qa_pairs.answer: Countering impunity of users, exploiters, and profit-makers is presented as a priority of the EU's response to trafficking. This shifts focus from victims alone to those generating and benefiting from demand.

qa_pairs.question: How does the passage connect the EU's anti-trafficking response to the Security Union framework?
qa_pairs.answer: It situates a new EU strategy on the eradication of trafficking in human beings, and an EU strategy on a more effective fight against child sexual abuse, as part of the Security Union. Trafficking is thereby framed as both a gender-equality concern and an internal-security concern.

qa_pairs.question: Why does the passage insist that the concerns of women and girls be at the centre of trafficking policy?
qa_pairs.answer: Because women and girls form the vast majority of victims of trafficking and are mostly trafficked for sexual exploitation, the passage argues their concerns must be at the centre of policy development. Centring victims is presented as a condition for effective and comprehensive policy.

chunks.content:
Convention on combating violence and harassment in the world of work, implement the existing EU rules on protecting workers from sexual harassment, and raise people's awareness of them. As an employer, the Commission will adopt a new comprehensive legal framework with a set of both preventive and reactive measures against harassment in the workplace.
Online violence targeting women has become pervasive with specific, vicious consequences; this is unacceptable. It is a barrier to women's participation in public life. Bullying, harassment and abuse on social media have far-reaching effects on women's and girls' daily lives. The Commission will propose the **Digital Services Act** to clarify online platforms' responsibilities with regard to user-disseminated content. The Digital Services Act will clarify what measures are expected from platforms in addressing illegal activities online, while protecting fundamental rights. Users also need to be able to counter other types of harmful and abusive content, which is not always considered illegal but can have devastating effects. To protect women's safety online, the Commission will facilitate the development of a new framework for cooperation between internet platforms.
Women and girls form the vast majority of victims of trafficking in human beings, both in and outside the EU, and are mostly trafficked for the purposes of sexual exploitation. The EU addresses trafficking in human beings comprehensively through coordination in all relevant areas. Countering impunity of users, exploiters and profit-makers is a priority. The concerns of women and girls affected by trafficking have to be at the centre of policy development. As part of the Security Union, the Commission will present a new EU strategy on the eradication of trafficking in human beings and an EU strategy on a more effective fight against child sexual abuse.
